title: 2 Hidar — St. Job the Patriarch & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "2 Hidar — St. Job the Patriarch & The Assembly

narrative": "title\": \"2 Hidar — St. Job the Patriarch & The Assembly

narrative\": \"Hidar

St. Job the Patriarch (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Peter of Alexandria, Pope and Martyr (Departure)

St. Habakkuk the Prophet

The second day of Hidar brings St. Job into the month of the Apostolic Vesting — and Job's vesting, in Hidar's register, is the vesting that happens after the stripping. The community that has come through the Pagumen washing and the Meskerem golden light and the Tiqimt interior disciplines has been, in a sense, progressively stripped of what was merely assumed and peripheral. Job lost everything external and in losing it discovered what was actually his: the encounter with God in the whirlwind, the word that came not as explanation but as presence, the restoration that was not merely the return of what had been taken but the revelation of what had always been given. Job's apostolic vesting is the vesting of those who have nothing left to prove — who can serve the community without the anxiety of the one still establishing himself, because the whirlwind has already settled the question.

St. Peter of Alexandria was one of the most significant bishops in the history of the early Church — the Pope of Alexandria who shepherded his community through the Great Persecution, who issued the Canonical Letter governing the reception of the lapsed back into the Church with a pastoral wisdom that the tradition preserved as a standard for centuries, and who was finally martyred in 311 A.D., one of the last martyrs of the persecution before Constantine's victory brought it to a close. His departure — his martyrdom — is commemorated on this second day of Hidar as the completion of an episcopal vesting that was both pastoral and theological. Peter understood that the bishop's apostolic commission is a commission to the whole community — not only to the strong and the faithful, but to the lapsed, the confused, and the partially faithful, who are still the Lord's.

St. Habakkuk the Prophet brings to the second day of Hidar one of the most searching prophetic dialogues in the entire Hebrew Bible — the prophet who dares to argue with God about the scandal of unpunished injustice, who refuses the easy answer, and who receives from God a response that is not a resolution of the scandal but a deepening of the covenant: 'The righteous shall live by faith.' This word — which Paul would later use as the cornerstone of the entire theology of justification — is the word of the apostolic vesting. The community is not sent out equipped with certainty about every question it will encounter; it is sent out equipped with the faith that will sustain it in the encounter with every question. The righteous live by this, and not by the answers.

Job, Peter of Alexandria, and Habakkuk together on the second day of Hidar present three modes of the apostolic vesting in its theological depth. Job is vested with the knowledge of the divine presence that the whirlwind brings — a presence more real than the theological explanations that preceded it. Peter is vested with the pastoral wisdom to shepherd the broken back to wholeness — the bishop's commission to the community's most difficult members. Habakkuk is vested with the prophetic formula that will anchor the faith of every subsequent generation in the place where certainty runs out. These are the tools of the apostolic month, and the community receives them on its second day with gratitude for the hard formation that prepared it to carry them.
